title: SEGMENT 14 — The cell-line landscape — CHO, HEK293, Pichia, plants

lines.text: SEGMENT 14 — The cell-line landscape — CHO, HEK293, Pichia, plants
Let me spend a segment on the cell lines, because the choice of host cell is one of the deepest and most consequential decisions in any biologic development program.
And it is, at its core, a glycobiology decision.
Different cell types make different glycans.
They make them because they have different repertoires of glycosyltransferases, different Golgi architectures, different substrate availability.

The same antibody gene, expressed in different host cells, will come out with different glycoform distributions, and those distributions will affect both the function and the immunogenicity of the resulting drug.
The dominant host cell, as I mentioned in Segment 6, is CHO — the Chinese hamster ovary line.
CHO is the workhorse.
It produces antibody at high titer, it folds proteins well, it scales reliably, and its glycosylation pattern is reasonably human-like.
But CHO does add some non-human sugars under certain conditions.
Notably, CHO can add a small amount of alpha-Gal — galactose-alpha-1,3-galactose — to the antibody glycan in some clones.

Alpha-Gal is an epitope that humans have natural antibodies against (because we lost the alpha-1,3-galactosyltransferase gene in primate evolution), and even small amounts of alpha-Gal on a therapeutic antibody can trigger anaphylactic reactions in sensitized patients.

The classic case is cetuximab, an anti-EGFR antibody produced in murine SP2/0 cells, which has alpha-Gal on its Fab glycan and caused severe hypersensitivity reactions in certain populations (predominantly in the Southeastern United States, where tick bites had pre-sensitized many people to alpha-Gal).
CHO also produces a non-human sialic acid called NGNA — N-glycolylneuraminic acid — in addition to the human form, NANA — N-acetylneuraminic acid.
Humans lack the enzyme that makes NGNA, and we have antibodies against it.
Most CHO-made antibodies have low NGNA, so it's usually not a clinical problem, but it's something manufacturers monitor.
HEK293 — human embryonic kidney 293 — is the alternative many companies have considered.
The appeal of HEK293 is that, being a human cell line, its glycosylation is fully human: no alpha-Gal, no NGNA.
The downsides are that HEK293 is generally less robust in suspension culture, has lower titers, and has been historically less characterized by regulators.
But for some specialty applications — particularly Fc fusion proteins where glycan fidelity is critical, or biologics where the target is glycan-sensitive — HEK293 has its place.
Pichia pastoris, the methylotrophic yeast, can be glycoengineered to produce humanized glycoproteins.

Companies like GlycoFi (acquired by Merck) developed Pichia strains in which the native yeast glycosylation machinery — which makes hypermannosylated structures unsuitable for human therapeutics — was systematically replaced with the human enzymes.
The engineered Pichia can make antibodies with defined human-type glycoforms, including afucosylated ones, at high titer.
The platform is real but has had limited clinical impact so far.
Plants — including tobacco, moss, and rice — have been engineered to make therapeutic antibodies.
The most famous example is ZMapp, the experimental anti-Ebola antibody cocktail produced in tobacco plants by Mapp Biopharmaceutical.
Plant glycosylation is fundamentally different from mammalian: plants add xylose and core alpha-1,3-fucose, both of which are foreign to mammals.

But plant lines can be engineered to lack these modifications, and indeed the resulting plant-made antibodies tend to be naturally afucosylated (lacking the mammalian core alpha-1,6-fucose), giving them enhanced ADCC.
Elelyso (taliglucerase alfa) for Gaucher disease is a plant-made glycoenzyme that demonstrates the platform can succeed clinically.
Here is the analogy I want you to hold for this segment.
CHO is the workhorse but produces non-human glycoforms; HEK293 is more human-like but less robust; plant systems give entirely different glycans; the cell line shapes the drug.
No host is perfect.
Each comes with a glycosylation fingerprint that you have to live with or engineer around.
And the choice of host is often the choice that most strongly determines the final glycoform of the product.
For a glycoengineered drug specifically, the host choice is even more loaded.
You need a host that can be modified to produce the target glycoform — afucose, hyper-galactosylated, sialylated, whatever — at high homogeneity.
CHO is the most engineerable host today, which is why the major glycoengineering platforms (Potelligent, GlymaxX) are CHO-based.
But for specialty glycoforms — fully sialylated, defined high-mannose, defined hybrid — alternative hosts or post-production enzymatic remodeling may be the better route.
The cell-line decision is rarely made in isolation.
It's made jointly with the glycoengineering strategy, the manufacturing scale, the indication, the regulatory pathway, and the cost-of-goods target.
But every one of those constraints comes back, eventually, to the question: what glycan does this cell produce, and is it the glycan I want?
